data.title: Why voters perceive Trump as 'awesome' — and what Democrats can learn from it
data.description:
Recently, I came across a report that did a better job explaining President Trump’s unique connection to certain voters than anything I have encountered since he first came to power. Yet it never even mentions the president’s name. In fact, it was published 12 years before Donald Trump first declared for the Republican nomination.

I’m talking about a 2003 paper written by Jonathan Haidt and Dacher Keltner for the journal Cognition and Emotion: “Approaching awe, a moral, spiritual, and aesthetic emotion.” By explaining the psychology behind awe, it helps answer the most frequently asked question in American politics: How does Trump — a billionaire who brazenly exploits his presidency to pad his net worth, adorns the Oval Office in gold, and affixes his name to the marble facades of government buildings — manage to connect with so many voters struggling in the shadows? How does he appeal so successfully to lower-income voters when he raises these voters’ bills through inflationary tariffs and expensive wars?

You may think the answer is simple, but it goes deep — to the areas of the brain that activate emotion. It turns out that many voters experience the same neurophysiological response that we get when we observe a majestic landscape, a feat of athletic prowess, or a spiritual awakening.

He triggers awe.

Haidt and Keltner’s paper is a relatively early contribution to the study of awe as an emotion — a field of research that has proven especially relevant in today’s politics. (Full disclosure, I have been studying awe recently as chairman of the Birch Creek Dialogues, which explores how we can leverage the power of awe to enhance civic engagement.) In a nation divided by ideology, the intangible quality of a candidate’s ability to command awe may be a greater factor in winning elections than we previously realized.

In Haidt and Keltner’s framework, there are two criteria that must be satisfied to induce awe as an emotion. First, something so vast — bigger than ourselves — that it diminishes our own self-perception. Second, an experience that challenges or negates our mental structures as we accommodate to make sense of it. When combined, the experiences can trigger a neurophysiological response that widens and glistens your eyes, lifts your head, sends shivers across your skin, reshapes your mouth to emit a breathless, “Whoa!”

How does Trump fit into that framework? Are we supposed to consider him in the same light as the awe induced by seeing the Grand Canyon, the aurora borealis, or Albert Bierstadt’s painting “Among the Sierra Nevada, California” in the Smithsonian? Well, Trump’s awe is different, but it is equally powerful. It stems from “primordial awe.”

Haidt and Keltner built on the work of others to propose that the primordial form of awe is the individual’s feeling toward a vastly more powerful other. In simple terms, it stems, in part, from our tribal origins, when survival required cohesion and cohesion demanded hierarchical leadership. “This feeling is likely to involve reverence, devotion, and the inclination to subordinate one’s own interests and goals in deference to those of the powerful leader,” they write.

When Trump wields his bombastic rhetoric from his gold-adorned Oval Office; as cranes construct his new ballroom nearby; as he commands masked and armed paramilitary forces that sweep into our cities, he cultivates a sense of awe in his supporters that reinforces his perch at the top of the hierarchy of American politics.

Trump’s awe has proven so powerful because his arrival to the political scene coincided with large swaths of the electorate feeling vulnerable. They were threatened by a unique convergence of events: 9/11, the 2008 economic meltdown, the implosion of previously trusted institutions (scandals that rocked sports, churches and banks), the rise of social media, the technological sea-change of automation and artificial intelligence, the pressures of increasing migration and globalization, and a devastating global pandemic.

When we feel most vulnerable, we turn to leaders who represent strength and authority: Leaders who will protect us (or our stock portfolios) invoke a sense of awe.

Trump isn’t the only politician who’s been propelled to office by inducing awe among supporters. President Barack Obama was the last Democrat on the national stage who managed to project awe, with his commanding oratory and charismatic presence. Elsewhere, in international politics, look to larger-than-life figures such as Giorgia Meloni, who is poised to become the longest continuously serving post-war Italian prime minister, and Volodymyr Zelensky, whose powerful voice has helped steer Ukraine through the crisis provoked by Russia’s unlawful invasion.

As Democrats try to define what voters think about Trump’s presidency, we cannot make the mistake of reducing voters’ attitudes to a discrete set of poll-tested policy leanings. Campaigns are about polls and points and data; but it’s often the secret sauce that wins. What counts is not how voters think about Trump’s ideological positioning — or that of Vice President JD Vance, Secretary of State Marco Rubio, or whoever else might emerge as Trump’s MAGA successor — it is what emotions are motivating their behavior. 

Yes, a leader’s policies still do matter. There are, of course, countless variables that go into winning a national election. But in this era of politics, one thing is clear: The candidate that inspires awe in voters is the one that can win awesome victories at the ballot box.

Steve Israel represented New York in the House of Representatives for eight terms and was chairman of the Democratic Congressional Campaign Committee from 2011 to 2015.

data.title: Mark Kelly on Artemis II mission: 'This is something we've never done before'
data.description:
Sen. Mark Kelly (D-Ariz.), a retired NASA astronaut, said Wednesday that the Artemis II mission to the moon is uncharted territory for American space explorers. 

“They’re going to go 4,000 miles on the far side of the moon, which is something we’ve never done before, even during Apollo,” Kelly told NewsNation’s Henry Amor at Kennedy Center Space Center in Florida, while donning his NASA uniform and a camo Navy hat. 

“The Apollo crews went in low moon, low lunar orbit,” he added. “So this is something we’ve never done before.”

Kelly and his identical twin, Scott Kelly, are the first and only siblings to have traveled into orbit — although they never did it together. Mark Kelly, who retired from NASA in 2011, has spent more than 50 days in space and traveled more than 20 million miles, according to his Senate website. 

The Artemis II test flight is scheduled for 6:24 p.m. ET on Wednesday evening, a trip that will mark the first crewed mission to head toward the moon since the final Apollo flight in December 1972. It will follow the uncrewed Artemis I moon-orbiting mission in 2022. 

The Arizona senator said that while he is “incredibly excited” for the mission, he “can’t imagine” he is “nearly as excited” as crew members Victor Glover, Jeremy Hansen, Christina Koch and Reid Wiseman.

Koch, an astronaut since 2013, said during a Sunday news conference that “things are certainly starting to feel real,” according to The New York Times. Ahead of the mission, Koch and her fellow crew members are in quarantine under “strict health monitoring,” and are completing medical checks to “ensure fitness for launch,” according to a Monday release from NASA.

The 10-day mission will take the astronauts around the moon, during which NASA will test the Orion spacecraft’s life support systems for the first time with people and “lay the groundwork” for future crewed Artemis missions, NASA said in another Monday release.

“It’s a new kind of mission. I’m excited about it because this is, essentially, one of the steps we need to put U.S. astronauts back onto the surface of the moon,” Kelly remarked.

Prelaunch coverage is already underway on NASA’s YouTube channel. Full coverage will start at 12:50 p.m. ET on NASA+, the agency’s free streaming service. Coverage will continue on YouTube after Orion’s solar array wings deploy in space.

data.title: JetBlue raises checked baggage fees amid soaring fuel prices
data.description:
TAMPA, Fla. (WFLA) – JetBlue is raising its checked baggage fees, citing increased operational costs.

The move also comes amid rising economic uncertainty and spiking oil prices in the month since the U.S. and Israel attacked Iran.

“As we experience rising operating costs, we regularly evaluate how to manage those costs while keeping base fares competitive and continuing to invest in the experience our customers value,” JetBlue said in a statement.

The airline still allows passengers to bring on a free carry-on bag and a personal item. If they want to check a bag, the price will depend on whether or not they’re traveling during “peak travel dates.”

The following dates are considered “peak” times, according to JetBlue:

  • April 2026: 4/2-4/27
  • May 2026: 5/21-5/25
  • June 2026: 6/11-6/30
  • July 2026: 7/1-7/31
  • August 2026: 8/1-8/31
  • September 2026: 9/1-9/9
  • October 2026: 10/10-10/14
  • November 2026: 11/19-11/30
  • December 2026: 12/17-12/31
  • January 2027: 1/1-1/18
  • February 2027: 2/11-2/22
  • April 2027: 4/1-4/26
  • May 2027: 5/20-5/24

On those dates, travelers will pay $49 to check a bag, up from $40. During non-peak times, the cost of checking a bag is $39, up from $35.

If paying to check a bag less than 24 hours before departure, customers will also be charged an additional $10 fee.

“Adjusting fees for optional services used by select customers, such as checked baggage, allows us to continue offering more competitive fares while delivering the onboard experience our customers love, including complimentary snacks and drinks, unlimited, high-speed Wi-Fi and seatback entertainment screens,” JetBlue’s statement read. “While we recognize that fee increases are never ideal, we take careful consideration to ensure these changes are implemented only when necessary.”

There are some exemptions to the checked baggage fee, such as customers with JetBlue credit cards or those with elite status in the airline’s loyalty program.

More information about JetBlue’s checked bag policy can be found on the airline’s website.

data.title: Live updates: Trump upends tradition, attends Supreme Court arguments on birthright citizenship
data.description:
The Supreme Court on Wednesday is weighing President Trump’s executive order seeking to end birthright citizenship.

The president is in the courtroom, meaning he is believed to be the first sitting president to attend oral arguments. At least twice this week he has ripped into the 14th Amendment’s citizenship guarantee.

At 9 p.m. EDT, Trump will address the nation from the White House on the Iran conflict. On Tuesday, he said he expected that U.S. troops would exit the region in about “two weeks.”

On Capitol Hill, the Senate’s bill to fund the Department of Homeland Security is gaining favor with the president.

Meanwhile, a push to end the ongoing U.S. military operation in Iran is gaining steam with House lawmakers.

Trump on Wednesday will attend an Easter lunch after oral arguments.

Follow live below.

data.title: Bring memorization back to schools
data.description:
I remember the first time I made my middle school students memorize poetry. I was plagued by indecision — still ringing in my ears were lectures from my education school professors castigating “rote memorization.”

I struggled to justify to my students why they ought to commit Blake or Shakespeare to memory, and, what’s more, recite their poems before the class.

This assignment, though planted in indecision, quickly bore convincing fruit. The students expressed a depth of analysis I hadn’t before seen. I noticed them later mimicking the poets’ complex vocabulary and syntax in their own writing.

But I was most surprised by social and cultural benefits. At recess, students who chose the same poem practiced them together on the swings. An otherwise meek little girl found her voice and recited her poem proudly before her peers. On presentation day, for one hour, we traded a classroom for Dickinson’s New England, Wordsworth’s Lake District, and Frost’s snowy wood.

Although I was surprised to see all these benefits, I shouldn’t have been. When today’s education “experts” dismiss rote memorization, they are ignoring wisdom that had been passed down for millennia. For most of history, what to me felt like a radical departure from assumed educational wisdom would have been as commonplace as classroom chairs — a part of the educational furniture.

For instance, throughout the Middle Ages, grammar schools began with letter sounds and memorization of the Psalms for practice. To be “psalteratus” — that is, to have memorized all 151 psalms — was considered synonymous with literacy.

Later, during the Renaissance, declamations, or recited passages, were centerpieces of the Jesuits’ Ratio Studiorum, an early standardizing document of educational best practices and regulations. From Athenian Grammar Schools to the American one-room schoolhouse, to know long passages of verse or great speeches by heart would have been no more extraordinary than a modern student writing a five-paragraph essay.

But two historical developments — one technological and one philosophical — have since brought memorization into disrepute.

First, in the fifteenth century, the advent of the printing press limited our need for memorization. Before mass printing, memorization was the prime means by which anyone could know an author. Who needs a library when students can shelve pages of arguments, verse, and prose in their minds? All of this changed when mass printing made it easier to purchase and read Homer than memorize him.

Second, in the twentieth century, educational progressives questioned the classical assumptions of western schooling. What is inherently worthy about Shakespeare or any other author, they asked, and who am I to tell a child that he or she ought to memorize them? Later radicals would rebuke traditional curricula not merely as anachronistic but even as downright racist, sexist, and hegemonic. To require a student to memorize a classic poem became an act of curricular oppression.

But the removal of memorization from our schools has been a serious mistake. From a purely utilitarian standpoint — before we even get to poetry — the memorization of the basic academic content is a necessary condition for all advanced thought. For example, the memorization of phonemes — until recently a practice that the self-anointed education experts looked down upon — allows for fluid reading. Likewise, the memorization of math facts facilitates easier computation in higher level procedures, and factual knowledge facilitates more complex reading comprehension and analysis.

But memorization of great literature gave my students more than utilitarian outcomes. It gave them pieces of their cultural inheritance. As students will inherit our society — its buildings, laws, norms, traditions, and government — so too they will inherit our most beautiful, compelling, and profound literary works. Educators are responsible for equipping students with the ability to preserve and cherish this inheritance.

Through memorization, students begin to mimic the best that is in authors, employing their patterns of thought, lines of argumentation, and knowledge in their own thinking and academic work. Such engagement awakens their minds, sharpens their reason, and forms their character.

When I was still teaching, as the school grounds filled up with snow, students came to visit me during my office hours and practiced their poems, sometimes smiling, sometimes frowning with earnest concentration. Among the students, it became the most anticipated assignment that I gave all year.

While contemporary discourse appends the modifier “rote” to memorization, we once called this practice “learning by heart.” As we hold dear a late father’s letter or a grown child’s baby blanket, so too beautiful verse becomes a cognitive keepsake — one to call forth when in a vacant or pensive mood, enjoying the bliss of solitude.

Daniel Buck is a research fellow at the American Enterprise Institute.

data.title: Katie Zacharia leaves DHS after weeks as spokesperson
data.description:
Katie Zacharia on Tuesday said she was leaving her role as a spokesperson for the Department of Homeland Security (DHS) and deputy assistant of public affairs after being assigned the role in mid-February. 

“I joined @DHSgov with a genuine passion for supporting @realDonaldTrump @POTUS in his decisive and effective policies to secure our homeland, be a voice for the precious Angel Families, and an advocate for the men and women of @ICEgov,” Zacharia wrote in a post on X. 

“I look forward to continuing that work in other capacities,” she added. 

The frequent Fox News commentator was known as a hardline Trump supporter prior to joining DHS. 

Zacharia followed Tricia McLaughlin, who left her role as a top spokesperson and assistant secretary for public affairs following scrutiny over the administration’s immigration tactics. 

The Daily Beast reported that Zacharia was ousted due to her close ties with special government employee Corey Lewandowski, who’s been accused of having an affair with former Homeland Security Secretary Kristi Noem and potentially benefiting from government contracts. 

Last month, House Democrats heightened their investigation into Lewandowski following accusations of mismanagement and corruption.

data.title: Trump approval on economy drops to new low: Poll
data.description:
A new CNN/SSRS poll found just 31 percent of respondents approved of President Trump’s handling of the economy, a new low relative to the outlet’s previous surveys.

The survey, released Wednesday, found that the president’s approval rating on the economy is 8 percent lower than it was in a January poll conducted by CNN/SSRS. The latest survey also found that more than three-quarters of respondents believe that economic conditions in the U.S. are poor, while 65 percent of respondents said Trump’s policies have worsened economic conditions — marks that are 8 and 10 points higher than they were in January, respectively. 

Even among Republicans, the president’s approval rating on the economy is down 14 points relative to January. Among Republicans younger than 45, his approval rating on the economy is down 23 points from the first month of the year.

Nearly 3 in 10 Republicans, in fact, said Trump’s policies have worsened economic conditions, up from the 13 percent who said so in January.

The polling comes as gas prices continue to increase amid the U.S.-Israeli war with Iran, surpassing $4 per gallon on Tuesday for the first time since 2022, according to AAA. In response to the initial wave of strikes by the U.S. and Israel, the Iranian government limited shipping through the Strait of Hormuz, a move that has had ripple effects for global energy prices. 

The president will address the nation at 9 p.m. EDT Wednesday regarding the war. It comes as the Pentagon deploys more troops to the Middle East and Trump touts talks with Iranian officials — discussions that Iranian leaders have publicly downplayed. 

Other data show that the economy has evolved slightly since Trump returned to office in January 2025. 

The inflation rate stayed at 2.4 percent in February, the same mark as the previous month and six-tenths of a percent lower than it was the month of the president’s inauguration, according to the Bureau of Labor Statistics (BLS).

The civilian unemployment rate, meanwhile, was 4.4 percent as of February, up 0.4 percent from January 2025, according to the BLS. 

The CNN/SSRS poll was conducted from March 26-30 via online or phone interviews with 1,201 U.S. adults. It has a margin of error of 3.2 percent.

data.title: Trump losing support from core supporters as approval drops to record low in new polling
data.description:
President Trump’s net approval dropped to a new low in this week’s YouGov/The Economist poll, fueled by declining support from the president’s core backers. 

The latest survey shows Trump’s net approval sinking to -23 points — with 35 percent of Americans approving and 58 percent disapproving of Trump’s handling of the presidency. 

That marks a notable decline from the net approval of -18 points last week, net -19 points the week before and net -15 points a week earlier. The net approval is also markedly lower than former President Biden’s net -6 percentage point approval at this point in his presidency and Trump’s net -11 percentage point approval at this point in his first term.

Biden’s worst net approval rating was also -23 percentage points, a low point recorded only twice in his presidency, both during his final year in office. 

Trump’s approval and disapproval ratings are also near his records. YouGov and The Economist only recorded a lower approval rating for Trump once during both terms — when 34 percent of Americans approved of his performance in November 2017. 

His disapproval rating similarly only surpassed current levels once — when 59 percent disapproved in February 2026.

The decline in Trump’s approval rating is largely driven by a softening of support from Trump’s own base, which has come amid a ramping up of U.S. military action in Iran, a partial government shutdown and a decline in the stock market.

The latest survey shows, among 2024 Trump voters, 76 percent approve of his handling of the presidency, compared to 19 percent who disapprove. That net +57 approval marks a 15-point drop from three weeks ago, when 84 percent approved and 12 percent disapproved. 

The latest survey marks a new second-term low for Trump’s net approval among Americans 65 and over, who’ve registered a net -17 percentage point approval of the president, with 57 percent disapproving and 40 percent approving of his handling of his job in office. Last week, net approval was -10 points, after starting this presidency at net -1 percentage points. 

Voters under 30 have also seen a sharp drop in support, with net approval at -40 percentage points this week, down from net -25 points last week, -39 percentage points the week before and net -29 points the week before that. 

Young voters recorded their lowest net approval of -54 percentage points in October 2025, after beginning his second term with a net approval of +5 percentage points. 

The survey, conducted on March 27-30, included 1,679 respondents and had a margin of error of 3.2 percentage points.

data.title: King Charles to address Congress during visit to DC
data.description:
King Charles III will address a joint session of Congress when he visits the U.S. later this month to mark the 250th anniversary of America’s independence, House Speaker Mike Johnson (R-La.) announced on Wednesday.

“It is my distinct honor and great privilege to invite His Majesty King Charles III, The King of the United Kingdom of Great Britain and Northern Ireland, to address a Joint Meeting of Congress,” Johnson wrote on the social platform X, describing the U.S.-U.K. relationship as “one of the most consequential partnerships in history.”

The King and Queen Camilla are set to visit Washington from April 27 to April 30, during which they will attend a state dinner at the White House.

In a letter to the King, congressional leaders suggested a joint address would provide Charles an opportunity to outline his vision for the future and “reaffirm our alliance at this pivotal time in history.”

“As we celebrate this historic milestone and recommit ourselves to the principles upon which our nation was founded, we also recognize that the American experiment endures in no small part because of the British tradition from which it sprang,” they wrote.

The historic trip — Charles’s first state visit to the U.S. as king — may be overshadowed by simmering tensions between Trump and British Prime Minister Keir Starmer over the war with Iran, however.

Trump has regularly singled out the U.K. when criticizing the reluctance of European allies to support the U.S. military operation in the Middle East, repeatedly lashing out at Starmer.

“This is not Winston Churchill that we’re dealing with,” Trump quipped to reporters in the White House early on in the conflict.

Some members of the British Parliament have demanded that Starmer scrap the state visit to demonstrate the country’s disapproval of Trump’s rhetoric and avoid embarrassment for the king, according to The Associated Press.

Ed Davey, leader of the opposition Liberal Democrats, told Sky News on Tuesday that the U.K. should not be “rewarding” Trump.

“You know, the truth is, he’s insulted our country, insulted our troops. He’s undermined our economy with his tariffs and this illegal war, which is putting up people’s petrol prices and energy bills and mortgage costs,” Davey said.

“And I don’t, frankly, think it’s fair to the king. He’s so unpredictable, Donald Trump. He could criticize our country again. He could criticize the government and leave the king in a very embarrassing situation. So I think this is the wrong decision,” the opposition party leader added.

Charles’s mother, the late Queen Elizabeth II, was the first monarch to address a joint session of Congress, doing so in May 1991.

data.title: Starmer says UK will form 'closer partnership' with Europe after Iran war strains US ties
data.description:
British Prime Minister Keir Starmer on Wednesday said the United Kingdom will form a “closer partnership” with Europe as ties with the U.S. have strained over the course of its conflict with Iran.

The prime minister said at a press conference that his government will support U.K. interests while guiding “our country calmly through this storm.”

“However, it is increasingly clear that as the world continues down this volatile path, our long-term national interest requires closer partnership with our allies in Europe and with the European Union,” the prime minister added.

Starmer said Brexit “did deep damage to our economy” and that its impact on the United Kingdom’s cost of living “are simply too big to ignore.”

“So, in the coming weeks, we will announce a new summit with our EU partners,” Starmer added. “And I can tell you that at that summit, the U.K. will not just ratify existing commitments made at last year’s summit. We want to be ambitious. Closer economic cooperation. Closer security cooperation.”

“A partnership that recognizes our shared values, our shared interest and our shared future. A partnership for the dangerous world that we must navigate together,” he added.

Starmer initially held off from getting the U.K. involved in the Iran war, despite requests from President Trump. Starmer blocked his U.S. counterpart’s request in late February for U.S. planes to use U.K. bases for preemptive strikes on Tehran, which the prime minister worried could violate international law.

Trump later slammed Starmer and Spain’s governments earlier this month over their stances toward the conflict with Iran. Like the U.K., Spain refused to allow the U.S. to use its bases amid the operation against Iran. The president said Spain “has been terrible” and said he was “not happy with the U.K., either.”

“This is not Winston Churchill,” he said about Starmer at the time.

The prime minister later allowed the U.S. to use the Royal Air Force Fairford and Diego Garcia bases on the Chagos Islands for “defensive” strikes. The U.K. has also boosted its military assets by deploying additional troops and air defense systems to the region.

The U.K. left the EU in January 2020 following the country’s 2016 referendum on whether to remain or leave. Stanford University’s Institute for Economic Policy Research estimated last year that Brexit reduced the U.K.’s GDP by 6 percent to 8 percent and raised the unemployment rate from 3 percent to 4 percent.

“These large negative impacts reflect a combination of elevated uncertainty, reduced demand, diverted management time, and increased misallocation of resources from a protracted Brexit process,” Stanford researchers wrote.
